petitioner_background: The petitioner is an Egyptian native and citizen who is a professor of forensic entomology (the study of insects on corpses to help solve crimes). He earned a bachelor's in zoology and master's and doctoral degrees in entomology from a university in Egypt, and has worked as an assistant/associate professor at universities in Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Lebanon, and the United States. He claims to have introduced a molecular entomological approach using mitochondrial DNA sequence data to identify forensically important insects, has about 30 years of experience as a forensic entomology expert in U.S., Lebanese, and Egyptian judicial systems, and his recent research includes applying 'maggot therapy' to treat venous and diabetic ulcers. He sought classification under the EB-1 employment-based, first-preference immigrant visa category as a noncitizen with extraordinary ability.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of his 1996 receipt of an Egyptian scholarship to continue his doctoral studies in the United States. The petitioner described it as 'one of the highest honors in my field' and stated he received it for his 'exceptional skills and academic prowess in forensic entomology,' and as a 'professional milestone recognized and celebrated in academic circles.' He submitted a letter from the general director of the Egyptian ministry that issued the scholarship describing it as 'prestigious' and a statement from a U.S. university professor of biology and forensic entomology stating the scholarship 'reflects his academic excellence but also demonstrates national recognition of his ... contribution to the field of forensic entomology.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO withdrew the Director's finding that the scholarship failed because it was limited to doctoral students, noting USCIS policy has 'no specific requirement that an award be open to all members of the field, including the most experienced.' However, the AAO still found the petitioner did not meet this criterion for two other reasons. First, the record lacked sufficient independent, objective evidence corroborating the scholarship's purported national or international recognition — issuance by a national ministry does not automatically prove national recognition, and there was no evidence the scholarship was received publicly or that news or professional organizations reported the grant's issuance (citing Visinscaia v. Beers). Second, the petitioner did not demonstrate the scholarship was issued 'in the field of endeavor' because he competed for the grant with Egyptian doctoral students in other fields, and the ministry's letter did not state the scholarship was limited to students who had studied entomology.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the petitioner's membership in various Egyptian scientific associations. On appeal, he stated he had been a member of one association since 2007 and described its affiliation with a larger international organization granting membership to those 'who have made a notable contribution to forensic and legal medicine through practice, research, or training.' He provided copies of printouts from the international association's website.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO affirmed the Director's finding. The materials did not demonstrate that the Egyptian associations require outstanding achievements of members or judgments by recognized national or international experts. The AAO reviewed the international association's website and found its 'Membership Criteria' section (which the petitioner's evidence omitted) shows the organization elects members to one of three levels: 'Fellowship,' 'Foundation Member,' or 'Affiliate Member.' Only fellowship and foundation members are those 'who have made a notable contribution to forensic and legal medicine.' The record did not indicate the petitioner's membership level — if he is an affiliate member, the organization would not have required him to have made a notable contribution. Additionally, he did not demonstrate that 'a notable contribution' meets this criterion's standard for 'outstanding achievements.' Further, even if he had fellowship or foundation membership, neither the website nor the record demonstrated that recognized national or international experts judge the group's membership candidates.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Online research profiles of himself, copies of articles citing work he co-authored, and a document indicating his appearance on a podcast episode recorded by a Lebanese radio station. On appeal, the petitioner claimed his inability to obtain a podcast transcript, stating the episode was 'recorded as audio' and contained a mix of Arabic and English languages.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO affirmed the Director's finding. None of the evidence indicated that the articles or podcast included a 'substantial discussion' of the petitioner and his work — the articles merely cited, in footnotes and endnotes without further discussion, articles he co-authored. Contrary to USCIS policy, the record lacked a transcript of the podcast episode. The record also lacked evidence that the articles and podcast qualify as 'professional or major trade publications, or other major media,' as the petitioner did not submit information about the publications' intended audiences or relative circulations, readerships, or viewership. On appeal, his unsupported statement about the podcast transcript's unavailability did not sufficiently demonstrate its unavailability or the unavailability of other evidence such as statements from the host or radio station employees, and a certified translation of any Arabic language would still be required.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Proof of his performance as a judge of others' work in his field.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The record supported the Director's finding that the petitioner satisfied this criterion. This was the one criterion the Director found met, and the AAO did not disturb that finding.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Copies of articles the petitioner co-authored, evidence of others' citations to his articles, and letters from both independent experts and scientists who have taught, worked with, or studied with him. The letters described the petitioner's research as 'pioneering' and 'groundbreaking' and cited his research on mitochondrial DNA-based identification of forensically important insects. The petitioner claimed his work 'has led to substantial advancements and has been widely cited by experts in the relevant domain.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO affirmed the Director's finding. The letters failed to illustrate how any of the petitioner's articles are considered to be of major significance in the field. Evidence must include more than conclusory statements — per USCIS policy, submitted letters should specifically describe the person's contribution and its significance to the field. The letters cited his research but did not explain the work's significance or how it advances the forensic entomology field. Letters that merely make general and expansive statements regarding a petitioner and their accomplishments are generally not persuasive.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence showing the petitioner co-authored at least nine scholarly articles. He submitted copies of abstracts of his articles, his research profiles, articles citing his work, and letters on his behalf, including one from an independent expert — a vice president at a U.S. university division of agriculture, life, and veterinary sciences — who stated the petitioner 'has to his credit several publications in leading journals, pertinent to his field of endeavor.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO withdrew the Director's contrary finding. While the record contained little information about the journals that published the petitioner's articles, the abstracts, research profiles, articles citing his work, and supporting letters sufficiently demonstrated that his articles were published in professional journals in his field. A preponderance of the evidence demonstrated the petitioner's authorship of scholarly articles in professional publications in his field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence that the petitioner performed in leading/critical roles as a researcher and educator for various universities around the world. He submitted a letter from the acting dean of faculty at the Egyptian university stating the petitioner's 'exceptional expertise and dedication to his field were foundational in the university's decision to establish the forensic entomology program,' that the program's growth under his supervision led a Brazilian university to agree to exchange knowledge and resources with the Egyptian school, and that the petitioner consulted on high-profile criminal cases in Egypt and 'positioned Egypt as a hub of forensic science expertise in the region.' He has served as the sole assistant professor in the Egyptian university's forensic entomology department since its establishment in 2005. He also provided printouts of online information about the schools, including world academic rankings of the Egyptian school.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found, contrary to the Director, that a preponderance of the evidence showed the petitioner performed in critical roles for universities in Egypt and Lebanon by developing their forensic entomology departments. However, the AAO agreed with the Director that the petitioner had not sufficiently demonstrated the purported distinguished reputations of the Egyptian and Lebanese universities. 'Distinguished' means 'marked by eminence, distinction, or excellence.' The record did not explain how the submitted printouts established the universities' eminence, distinction, or excellence — the Egyptian school's world academic rankings did not appear high enough to demonstrate a distinguished reputation (citing Karim v. Allen). Because the universities' qualifying reputations were not sufficiently demonstrated, the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner met only two evidentiary criteria (judging, and authorship of scholarly articles) when at least three are required to proceed to a final merits determination. The AAO withdrew the Director's finding on scholarly articles in the petitioner's favor, but this still left him one criterion short of the minimum required under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). Because he did not satisfy the requisite number of evidentiary requirements, the AAO did not reach a final merits determination and affirmed the petition's denial.
legal_citations: Immigration and Nationality Act section 203(b)(1)(A), 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
Jama v. Immigr. & Customs Enf't, 543 U.S. 335 (2005)
Massachusetts Trs. of E. Gas & Fuel Assocs. v. United States, 377 U.S. 235 (1964)
Karim v. Allen, No. 21-cv-2861-WJM-KLM, 2023 WL 4624896 (D. Colo. July 19, 2023)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO declined to consider additional evidence submitted on appeal, citing 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(11) and Matter of Soriano, because the petitioner had a chance to submit the materials in response to the Director's request for evidence before denial. The AAO also noted that the petitioner's evidence for the membership criterion omitted the 'Membership Criteria' section of the international association's website, which the AAO independently reviewed and found undermined his claim. The AAO applied the harmless-error rule to withdraw (without reversing or remanding) the Director's erroneous finding on the awards criterion regarding scholarship eligibility limitations.
